plainMeaning: Bowing my head to all the other steadfast teachers who expounded the fixed-time scriptures, I will now describe knowledge itself.
recitation: je aṇṇo bhagavante, kāliya sūya aṇuogie dhīre. te paṇamiūṇa sirasā, nāṇassa parūvaṇaṁ vucchaṁ.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit relative pronoun je, from Skt ye (nom./acc. pl. of yad)
wordByWord.notes: Correlates with te in the third quarter: 'those who… them I bow to.'

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit aṇṇa, from Skt anya ('other'); the doubled ṇṇ is the regular Prakrit assimilation of -ny-
wordByWord.notes: The sweeping gesture: 'all the other' masters not individually named in the preceding verses.

wordByWord.gloss: the venerable ones, revered teachers (accusative plural)
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit bhagavanta, from Skt bhagavant ('possessing bhaga — fortune, majesty, glory')
wordByWord.notes: Here the honoured ācāryas of the lineage, not the Tīrthaṅkaras; object of paṇamiūṇa.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit kāliya, from Skt kālika (kāla, 'time' + -ika), 'pertaining to a fixed time'
wordByWord.notes: Forms the compound kāliya-sūya (kālika-śruta), the body of canon these masters preserved.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit sūya/suya, from Skt śruta ('that which is heard'), √śru
wordByWord.notes: Joined with kāliya as kālika-śruta; the genitive scope of the teachers' expertise.

wordByWord.gloss: (those) versed in the exposition, in the anuyoga (accusative plural)
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit aṇuogiya, from Skt ānuyogika / anuyoga-in ('concerned with anuyoga, systematic exposition')
wordByWord.notes: Qualifies bhagavante: teachers expert in the anuyoga of the kālika-śruta.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit dhīra, from Skt dhīra ('steady, resolute, wise'), from dhī ('intellect')
wordByWord.notes: A stock epithet of the ācāryas — unwavering both in conduct and in command of the canon.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit te, from Skt tān (acc. pl. of tad)
wordByWord.notes: Resumes je: 'those (just described) — them I salute.'

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit absolutive paṇamiūṇa, from Skt praṇamya (pra + √nam, 'to bow down')
wordByWord.notes: Gerund/absolutive (-iūṇa) — the act completed before the main verb vucchaṁ.
The bodily completeness of the bow — pañcāṅga-style reverence led by the head.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit nāṇa, from Skt jñāna ('knowledge'), √jñā; genitive -assa = Skt -asya
wordByWord.notes: Announces the Nandī's true subject, developed in the next verse as the five jñānas.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit parūvaṇā, from Skt prarūpaṇā (pra + √rūp, 'to set forth, expound')
wordByWord.notes: A technical anuyoga term for systematic exposition; object of vucchaṁ.

wordByWord.gloss: I shall speak, I shall expound (future, 1st sg.)
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit vucchaṁ, from Skt vakṣye (future of √vac, 'to speak')
wordByWord.notes: The hinge-word: the poet pivots from homage to the promised teaching.

literalTranslation: Those other venerable, steadfast ones, versed in the exposition of the fixed-time scripture — having bowed to them with my head, I shall now expound the description of knowledge.
idiomaticTranslation: And to all those other steadfast, venerable masters who expounded the kālika-śruta — bowing low to them with my head, I now turn to set forth the teaching on knowledge itself.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: This is the hinge of the whole hymn. Having named teacher after teacher through the preceding verses, the poet now gathers in everyone he has not named: 'je aṇṇo' — 'those others,' all the remaining guardians of the kālika-śruta who are equally worthy of homage. The qualification is precise: they are 'aṇuogie dhīre,' steadfast precisely in the anuyoga — the disciplined exposition — of the fixed-time canon. They are honoured not for fame but for the fidelity with which they transmitted what was entrusted to them. With 'te paṇamiūṇa sirasā' the long act of vandanā reaches its bodily climax: a single, comprehensive bow of the head that closes the lineage-roll. Then, mid-breath, the verse turns. 'Nāṇassa parūvaṇaṁ vucchaṁ' — 'I shall expound the description of knowledge' — is the poet's vow to move from the keepers to the kept, from those who guarded the teaching to the teaching itself. The structure enacts a deep propriety: one bows to the transmitters before daring to handle the transmission. Note that 'parūvaṇā' is not loose talk but a technical term of the anuyoga method — ordered, definitional setting-forth — which prepares the reader for the schematic five-fold enumeration that immediately follows. The verse thus does two things at once: it seals the Sthavirāvalī with a final collective salute, and it opens the doctrinal core of the Nandī.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse encodes the Jain pedagogical axiom that vinaya (reverent humility) precedes and conditions jñāna. Knowledge in this tradition is never self-generated; it is received down a living chain, so the head must bow before the mind may learn. By bowing to the 'aṇuogie' masters and then promising the 'parūvaṇā' of nāṇa, the poet aligns himself with the anuyoga method itself — the structured fourfold exposition by which śruta is unpacked — and signals that what follows (the five jñānas: mati/abhinibodhika, śruta, avadhi, manaḥparyaya, kevala) is offered as received doctrine, not personal opinion. The collective 'je aṇṇo' also expresses the humility of incompleteness: no succession-roll can be exhaustive, so the unnamed are honoured alongside the named.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: At this point in the Pakkhī recitation the worshipper, having honoured the lineage, performs the inner bow the verse describes and consciously shifts posture — from gratitude toward the teachers to attentiveness toward the teaching. The lesson for daily practice is direct: approach study (svādhyāya) by first lowering the head in reverence to those who preserved it, then take up the subject in that same humility.

crossReferences.text: Nandī Sūtra, the five-jñāna enumeration immediately following (abhinibodhika, śruta, avadhi, manaḥparyaya, kevala)
Tattvārtha Sūtra 1.9–1.12 — the fivefold classification of jñāna

sources.title: Jain eLibrary — Nandī Sūtra (Nandīsūtra)
sources.consultedFor: Verse placement as the transition from the Sthavirāvalī to the five-jñāna section and the reading nāṇassa parūvaṇaṁ vucchaṁ.

sources.title: Cologne Digital Sanskrit Lexicon (Monier-Williams)
sources.consultedFor: Sanskrit roots underlying the Prakrit tokens: praṇamya, śiras, prarūpaṇā, jñāna, vakṣye.

sources.title: Pāia-sadda-mahaṇṇavo (Prakrit–Sanskrit dictionary), H. T. Sheth
sources.consultedFor: Prakrit forms aṇṇa, sūya, aṇuogiya, parūvaṇā and the absolutive ending -iūṇa.

notes: The verse is a transition gāthā: 'je aṇṇo' gathers the unnamed remaining masters of the kālika-śruta anuyoga, and 'vucchaṁ' announces the turn to the five-jñāna exposition that v.27 begins. bhagavante here denotes the lineage ācāryas, not the Tīrthaṅkaras. aṇuogie/dhīre are read as accusative-plural epithets agreeing with bhagavante; some editions may construe aṇuogie locatively ('in the anuyoga'), which yields the same sense. parūvaṇaṁ (Skt prarūpaṇā) is rendered 'exposition/description,' a technical anuyoga term, not 'types.' Every token is glossed verbatim from the book's Gujarati script; no variant readings were flagged in the supplied text, though general witnesses show aṇuoge/aṇuogie spelling variation.
